steps.title: Identify the core judgment about ignorance
steps.content: The opening sentence makes the passage's main point explicit: “It was happy for the Rev. Amos Barton that he did not... overhear the conversation.” This directly establishes the passage's core claim—that not knowing what others think is beneficial.

steps.title: Match the judgment to the answer
steps.content: C states precisely what the passage argues: people are better off not knowing about the discrepancy between their self-image and others' actual perceptions. A, B, and D pursue unrelated topics—virtue hindered by setbacks, fixation on others' approval, and truthfulness about others' failings—that the passage never discusses. That matches choice C — C is correct.
